text:
The Prosiměřice 2 metal hoard was investigated through a multi-disciplinary approach using results obtained from a rescue excavation carried out in 2021 in South Moravia. The hoard was discovered in the Jevišovka River floodplain below the water table and consists of two socketed axes, nine copper and bronze ingots, a sprue, and a metal stick. By analysing the morphology of the axes, the Prosiměřice 2 hoard was dated back to the Late Bronze Age, specifically to the Reinecke period Ha B1/B2, which corresponds to the time of the Middle Danube Urnfield Culture. By applying the material studies’ method with ED-XRF, it was possible to determine the complexity of the metallurgical production at Prosiměřice including unrefined copper as well as finished tin-bronze alloys, which also points towards the existence of an active production chain within the primary settlement layer.

Preparation of axe blades for use, as deduced from the results of the X-ray computed tomography investigation combined with use-wear analysis, has been confirmed, despite the presence of obvious defects (large number of macro porosities and incompletely solidified areas) in the casting of the bronze axes. Information about the environment at the deposition site derived from pollen analysis and from archaeological maps and literature, also indicate that at the time of the deposition of the hoard, the site was an aqueous environment, probably either a wetland or open water. Using information about the settlement hierarchy of the region in which the axes were deposited, it is inferred that the axes of the hoard were products of a local metal production activity and that the ultimate disposal of these suitable for use, but perhaps inherently defective axes, in an irreversible and ritualistic manner, in an aqueous context of Bronze Age context, must be seen in the context of socio-cultural activities that connected economic and technological activities in the Aegean Bronze Age cultural landscape.

prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
